negative-numbers.name: Negative Numbers
negative-numbers.content:
What are negative numbers?

Negative numbers are any number below 0. At first, this idea might seem confusing, but they actually are really simple numbers.

Imagine you are facing forward. If you take 3 steps, now you are 3 steps ahead of your original position. Now if you take two steps back, you are now at position 1. This can be seen in the expression:

3 - 2 = 1

Other examples like this can be easily made. For example, you take 5 steps forward and 3 back. This is expressed like so:

5 - 3 = 2

And the final distance forward in steps is finally 2, as the math says.

But what happens when you take more steps back than you took forward? Is there a wall stopping you? (we're going to assume not) How do you know what is your position?

That's why we have negative numbers. Imagine you took 3 steps forward and 5 steps back. The expression that reflects this behaviour is:

3 - 5 = -2

This is an exact opposition to the other one, and you can compare by yourself.

5 - 3 = 23 - 5 = -2

As you can see, the 2 has a minus sign added to it. What does it mean? The -2 means the 2 steps back you are from you original position. Were it 2, it would be two steps forward. -2 then, means 2 steps back.

I really like using this meme to see how it works. It portrays Michael Jackson doing the moonwalk as the negative numbers. It will help us understand how to operate with negative numbers.

The Key Idea

To learn to understand substraction as a "turning around"

Cases

8 + 6

Pretty self-explanatory, so we will skip it.

8 - 6

Also self-explanatory, so we will also skip it.

8 + (-6)

We will start explaining this one before the one on the left because this one is easier. Imagine we take 8 steps forward, and then we go full Moonwalk mode and walk 6 steps more. The thing is, that the Moonwalk walks backward, so we would walk the 6 steps to the back instead of to the front. That is actually the same as just substracting it normally. It is important to instantly relate this:

a + (-b) = c

To:

a - b = c

Because they are always the same by definition. Let's give examples.

8 + (-6) = 28 - 6 = 2
13 + (-7) = 613 - 7 = 6
11 + (-3) = 811 - 3 = 8

8 - (-6)

scientific-notation.name: Scientific Notation
scientific-notation.content:
What is Scientific Notation?

It's a way of writing numbers that are too large to be written normally, using a multiplication between a decimal number and a power of 10. Some of its best uses find their place in science, hence "Scientific" Notation. Some examples


The mass of the Earth: 5972000000000000000000000kg

The mass of an animal cell: 0.000000000035kg

The distance between the Sun and Earth: 149600000000m

Carbon Atomic Radius: 0.00000000007m


Scientists find it very inconvenient to have that many 0, which have to be counted one by one in order to preserve accuracy.

What's the solution?

They came up with an idea. Use the versatile number 10, the base of our counting system to simplify the number calculations. If we remember from previous lessons, the powers of the number 10 behave like this:

10^{3}=1000
10^{2} = 100
10^{1}=10
10^{0} = 1
10^{-1}=0.1
10^{-2} = 0.01

Where the amount of zeros in each number is equal to the power (and grows backwards if the exponent is negative).

So, scientists used this quality to simply express large numbers. All of the scientific notation numbers have the same structure:

a \cdot 10^{k}

Where:

1 \le a \lt 10

And:

k \in \mathbb{Z}

Or, otherwise:

"A multiplication between any number greater or equal to 1 and smaller than 10 and an integer power of 10"

Examples

Now that we have the idea, let's give some examples to better understand. Let's say we have the number 8000000000 (nine zeros) and want to express it with scientific notation.

This is how we would do it:

8\color{Cyan}\underbrace{000000000}_{9}

That, by definition would be equal to this:

8\cdot 10^{\color{Cyan}9}

Let's reutilize the examples from earlier. First the mass of the Earth:

5\color{Cyan}\underbrace{\color{Magenta}\overbrace{972}^{\ne 0}\color{Cyan}000000000000000000000}_{24}\color{Black}kg=5.\color{Magenta}972\color{Black} \cdot 10^{\color{Cyan}24}kg

The distance between the Sun and Earth:

1\color{Cyan}\underbrace{\color{Magenta}\overbrace{496}^{\ne 0}\color{Cyan}00000000}_{11}\color{Black}kg=1.\color{Magenta}496\color{Black} \cdot 10^{\color{Cyan}11}m

The mass of an animal cell:

\color{Cyan}\underbrace{0.0000000000\color{Magenta}3}_{12}\color{Magenta}5\color{Black}kg=\color{Magenta}3.5\color{Black}\cdot 10^{-\color{Cyan}12}kg

And the Carbon Atomic Radius:

\color{Cyan}\underbrace{0.0000000000}_{11}\color{Magenta}7\color{Black}m=\color{Magenta}7\color{Black}\cdot 10^{-\color{Cyan}11}m
